title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Nocturnal Laboratory: Why We Dream
content.subtitle: Exploring the evolutionary and cognitive functions of our sleeping minds.
content.text: For centuries, dreams were regarded as either divine messages or meaningless biological byproducts—the 'noise' of a brain warming up for the day. However, modern neuroscience has repositioned REM (Rapid Eye Movement) sleep as a crucial period of cognitive maintenance. [31] Far from being a state of rest, the dreaming brain is often more metabolically active than the waking one, suggesting that dreaming is an essential, albeit mysterious, physiological requirement. [/31] It appears that while our bodies are paralyzed to prevent us from acting out our fantasies, our minds are performing complex data processing. || One prominent theory is that dreams serve as a 'threat simulation' mechanism. By placing us in frightening or high-stakes scenarios—such as being chased or failing an important task—the brain allows us to rehearse our responses in a safe environment. [32] This evolutionary perspective suggests that those who 'practiced' survival during their sleep were better equipped to handle real-world dangers, thereby increasing their chances of passing on their genes. [/32] It is, in essence, a virtual reality training ground that has been fine-tuned over millions of years. || [33] Beyond survival, dreaming plays a pivotal role in emotional regulation, acting as a form of 'overnight therapy.' [/33] During REM sleep, the brain is uniquely devoid of noradrenaline, a chemical associated with stress. This allows us to process traumatic or upsetting memories without the physiological 'sting' of the original event. By stripping away the emotional charge, the brain can integrate these experiences into our long-term memory, transforming a raw, painful episode into a manageable narrative. || [34] Critics of these functional theories argue that we are prone to 'apophenia'—the human tendency to find patterns in random information. [/34] They suggest that dreaming might simply be the mind's attempt to make sense of the random neural firing that occurs during sleep. From this viewpoint, the 'story' of a dream is a post-hoc construction, a narrative we weave upon waking to explain a chaotic sequence of internal images. However, this fails to account for the highly structured and repetitive nature of many dream themes. || [35] Recent studies into 'lucid dreaming'—the ability to become aware that one is dreaming while still asleep—have opened new avenues for research. [/35] By signaling to researchers through pre-arranged eye movements, lucid dreamers have proven that the brain can execute voluntary intentions while unconscious. This has led some to speculate that we might one day use sleep for intentional skill acquisition or creative problem-solving, turning the 'lost' hours of the night into productive time. || [36] Ultimately, the true purpose of dreaming may be a combination of these factors, forming a multifaceted system for memory consolidation and emotional survival. [/36] As we continue to peel back the layers of the nocturnal mind, we find that sleep is not the absence of life, but a different, equally vital form of it. To understand the dream is to understand the very machinery of human thought, reminding us that even in our darkest moments, our minds are working tirelessly to make sense of the world.

questions.question: What does the author note about the activity of the brain during REM sleep?
options: A) It is significantly lower than when we are awake to allow for recovery.
B) It proves that dreaming is merely a way for the brain to warm up.
C) It involves a high level of energy consumption despite the body's stillness.
D) It is primarily focused on controlling the movements of the body.
questions.explanation: The text states the dreaming brain is 'more metabolically active than the waking one,' indicating high energy use (metabolism) during sleep.

questions.question: The 'threat simulation' theory implies that dreaming...
options: A) is a byproduct of modern anxieties that did not exist in the past.
B) provided a competitive advantage to our ancestors.
C) is a malfunction of the brain's ability to distinguish reality.
D) prevents people from taking risks in their actual lives.
questions.explanation: The text says those who 'practiced' survival in dreams were 'better equipped to handle real-world dangers,' increasing their chances of survival and reproduction.

questions.question: How does dreaming function as 'overnight therapy'?
options: A) It completely erases negative memories from the long-term memory.
B) It uses stress chemicals to harden the mind against future trauma.
C) It allows the brain to process difficult emotions without a stress response.
D) It encourages people to talk about their problems upon waking.
questions.explanation: The text mentions the brain is 'devoid of noradrenaline' (stress chemical), allowing us to process memories without the 'physiological sting.'

questions.question: What is the main argument of the critics mentioned in the fourth paragraph?
options: A) Dreams are too chaotic to be studied by modern science.
B) The meaning we find in dreams is something we create after we wake up.
C) Neural firing during sleep is a sign of a healthy intellectual life.
D) Humans are the only species capable of creating narratives from dreams.
questions.explanation: They suggest the 'story' is a 'post-hoc construction'—a narrative we weave when we wake up to explain random signals.

questions.question: What has been proven by research into lucid dreaming?
options: A) That it is impossible to control one's actions while asleep.
B) That the unconscious mind can follow through on a conscious plan.
C) That eye movements are the primary cause of vivid dreams.
D) That skill acquisition only happens during the waking hours.
questions.explanation: Research showed dreamers could execute 'voluntary intentions' (signaling researchers) while still being unconscious.

questions.question: In the conclusion, the author's view of dreaming is that it is...
options: A) a single-purpose mechanism for memory storage.
B) a redundant process that humans will eventually outevolve.
C) an essential component of how we process and survive our experiences.
D) a mystery that will likely never be solved by neuroscience.
questions.explanation: The author describes it as a 'multifaceted system' for memory and 'emotional survival' that is 'vital' for human thought.
